name: Atlassian Corporation

profile.description: Atlassian Corporation provides enterprise collaboration and developer tools including Jira, Confluence, Trello, Bitbucket, and Jira Service Management. The company serves over 350,000 customers worldwide, including 75% of the Fortune 500. Founded in 2002 in Sydney, Australia by co-founders Mike Cannon-Brookes and Scott Farquhar, Atlassian is executing a cloud-first migration strategy and has launched Rovo, an AI agent platform now bundled for all enterprise customers. TTM revenue of $6.19B with 24.74% YoY growth.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Atlassian has successfully executed a multi-year transformation from on-premise and Data Center licensing to a cloud-first SaaS model, fundamentally reshaping its revenue profile. Cloud revenue crossed the $1B-per-quarter milestone at $1.132B in Q3 FY2026 (+29% YoY) [1]. The Rovo AI agent platform launched and achieved rapid adoption across the enterprise base, representing a genuine platform expansion beyond traditional collaboration tools [2]. AI Deployment (2/2): Rovo is shipping to customers with measurable, quantified results: agentic automation grew 7x in 6 months, 14M+ assisted actions in a single month, used by 75% of Fortune 500 and 90%+ enterprise customers [2]. Atlassian made the strategic decision to include Rovo free for all enterprise users, treating AI as an engagement and retention driver rather than a standalone revenue line. Employees across legal, engineering, and marketing are building their own AI agents — thousands of internal agents deployed across departments [2]. Market Share (2/2): Jira is the de facto standard for engineering project management, and Confluence dominates technical documentation. 350,000+ customers with significant switching costs — institutional knowledge, workflows, integrations, and compliance configurations are deeply embedded in the platform [1]. Category leader in developer collaboration tools with minimal direct substitution risk. Product Pipeline (1/2): Clear growth vectors include Rovo expansion, ongoing Data Center to Cloud migration pipeline, and cross-sell through Service Collection and Teamwork Collection bundles [1]. However, no entirely new product category has been announced — growth is primarily deepening existing platform capabilities rather than entering adjacent markets. R&D Intensity (1/1): Significant R&D investment appropriate for enterprise SaaS at this scale, achieving non-GAAP operating margin of 34% while investing heavily in AI and Rovo development [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Atlassian's mission 'to unleash the potential of every team' is specific, distinctive, and has demonstrably shaped product decisions for over 20 years since founding [2]. The mission directly connects to how the company builds products — Rovo was made free for enterprise users specifically because it serves the team empowerment mission, not because of a pricing strategy. Co-founder Cannon-Brookes's vision that 'intelligence needs context to create value' extends the mission into the AI era. The founding ethos has been consistent across the on-premise-to-cloud transition, leadership changes, and platform expansions. Not quite a 4/4 because the mission hasn't survived the kind of existential, bet-the-company crisis that tests whether a cause truly transcends strategy. Community Impact (1/2): Atlassian Foundation operates on the 1% pledge model — 1% of profit, 1% of product, and 1% of employee time donated to social causes [3]. The company donates software licenses and employee volunteer hours. Genuine community investment with a structured framework, but the dollar amounts are not extraordinary relative to company scale. Employee Retention (1/2): 13,813 employees operating under a founder-led culture with 'Open company, no bullshit' as a core value [2]. Long-tenured leadership team and generally positive employer reputation. However, no specific turnover or tenure data is publicly available to distinguish Atlassian from industry average. Insider Buying (1/2): Cannon-Brookes maintains a significant founder ownership stake, providing strong alignment between leadership and shareholders. Farquhar's transition to non-executive role maintained his equity position rather than triggering a selldown. Founder alignment is strong, though no notable open-market purchases were identified.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Atlassian's infrastructure profile reflects a high-growth enterprise SaaS company with GAAP metrics distorted by stock-based compensation and acquisition amortization. Revenue growth is exceptional: 24.74% TTM YoY and approximately 26% 5-year CAGR, both well above target thresholds [1][5]. Cloud revenue grew 29% YoY to $1.132B in Q3 FY2026 [1]. Forward P/E of 14.10x is remarkably low for a company growing revenue at 25%+, producing a non-GAAP PEG ratio of approximately 0.56 — deeply undervalued on a growth-adjusted basis [5]. D/E of 141% is elevated but manageable for enterprise SaaS. The GAAP metrics tell a misleading story: ROE of -19.29% and profit margin of -3.50% are driven by non-cash SBC expense, while non-GAAP operating margin stands at a healthy 34% [1]. Liquidity is exceptional at $571.5M average daily dollar volume (7.21M shares x $79.26), well above the $1M floor [5].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Atlassian occupies a PROTECTED position bordering on IMMUNE. Jira and Confluence are deeply embedded in enterprise workflows with massive switching costs — institutional knowledge, project histories, compliance configurations, and thousands of integrations are locked into the platform [1]. AI enhances rather than threatens these tools: Rovo makes Jira and Confluence more valuable by adding intelligent automation and search across existing team knowledge. The company IS deploying AI as a core feature rather than being disrupted by it. Regulatory and compliance requirements (SOC 2, HIPAA, FedRAMP) create additional moats that AI-native startups cannot easily replicate. However, the score stops short of 8-9/9 because AI-native project management and developer tools could theoretically emerge as competitors — tools like Linear, Notion AI, and GitHub Copilot Workspace nibble at different edges of Atlassian's market [2]. AI Opportunity (1/1): Rovo is generating significant, quantified AI-driven engagement: 14M+ assisted actions per month, 7x growth in agentic automation in 6 months, adoption by 75% of Fortune 500 and 90%+ enterprise customers [2]. AI is a clear revenue enabler through improved retention, deeper platform engagement, and enterprise upsell. The strategic decision to bundle Rovo free for enterprise users accelerates the AI flywheel — more agents built means more institutional knowledge captured, which increases switching costs.

infinite_game.summary: Atlassian demonstrates strong infinite-game characteristics rooted in 20+ years of founder-led, mission-consistent team collaboration tools. The decision to make Rovo AI free for all enterprise customers — sacrificing potential AI monetization to deepen platform value — exemplifies playing for long-term relevance over short-term revenue. The cloud migration, which initially cannibalized higher-margin Data Center revenue, showed willingness to endure near-term pain for structural improvement. Co-founder Cannon-Brookes's personal climate investments and the company's 'Open company, no bullshit' culture further signal infinite-game thinking.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): 'Unleash the potential of every team' has been Atlassian's north star for over 20 years, from the founding in Sydney through IPO, cloud migration, and now the AI era [2]. The cause visibly shapes product decisions — making Rovo free for all enterprise users, building tools that empower teams rather than control them, and co-founder Cannon-Brookes's insistence that 'intelligence needs context to create value' connects AI strategy directly to the team empowerment mission. The cause has remained consistent across leadership transitions (Farquhar stepping back), business model shifts (on-premise to cloud), and platform expansions (Rovo). A 2/3 rather than 3/3 because the company hasn't survived the kind of existential crisis that truly tests whether the cause transcends strategy.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (2/2): Atlassian's culture is distinctively trust-oriented, anchored by the core value 'Open company, no bullshit' — a value that has persisted since founding [2]. Employees across legal, engineering, and marketing departments are empowered to build their own AI agents without centralized approval, resulting in thousands of internal agents deployed — this represents genuine decentralized empowerment, not top-down mandates [2]. Co-founder Cannon-Brookes describes AI agents as 'virtual teammates,' extending the team-first philosophy to AI itself. The founder-led culture with long-tenured leadership provides stability, and the 13,813-person organization maintains the empowerment ethos despite enterprise scale.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Atlassian competes with Microsoft (Azure DevOps, Teams), ServiceNow, Monday.com, Asana, and emerging AI-native tools like Linear and Notion [2]. Cannon-Brookes acknowledges competitive strengths and uses them to drive innovation — the Rovo launch and cloud migration were responses to competitive pressure from Microsoft and emerging SaaS tools, approached with a learn-and-improve mindset rather than market-share obsession. The company's willingness to make Rovo free rather than charging premium AI pricing reflects confidence in its differentiation rather than fear of competitors.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The cloud migration was a significant strategic shift from on-premise licensing to cloud-first SaaS, requiring Atlassian to cannibalize its profitable Data Center business to build a structurally better revenue model [1]. Rovo represents a meaningful platform expansion from passive collaboration tools to active AI-powered team intelligence. Both demonstrate strategic adaptability. However, Atlassian has always been a collaboration software company serving developer and enterprise teams — there has been no truly transformative reinvention of the core identity or business model. The company has deepened its position rather than fundamentally pivoted.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Atlassian made Rovo available free for all enterprise customers — deliberately sacrificing potential AI revenue to drive platform adoption and deepen engagement [2]. This decision, when competitors like Microsoft and ServiceNow charge premium prices for AI features, demonstrates genuine conviction in long-term platform value over short-term monetization. The cloud migration commitment initially hurt short-term revenue as Data Center customers transitioned, but management stayed the course through multiple quarters of investor pressure [1]. Co-founder Cannon-Brookes's personal $1B+ investments in renewable energy and vocal climate leadership demonstrate courage extending beyond the corporate context [3]. The company sets net-zero emissions targets by 2040 with science-based 1.5C alignment — commitments with real cost [3].

investment_thesis: Atlassian at 14.1x forward earnings with 25%+ revenue growth represents one of the most attractively valued high-growth enterprise SaaS companies in the market [5]. Cloud revenue crossed $1.132B per quarter (+29% YoY), and the Rovo AI platform has achieved remarkable traction — 14M+ assisted actions per month, 7x growth in agentic automation, adoption by 75% of Fortune 500 [1][2]. The non-GAAP PEG ratio of approximately 0.56 suggests significant undervaluation relative to growth. With 350,000+ customers, deep enterprise switching costs, and a 34% non-GAAP operating margin, Atlassian combines durable competitive moats with AI-driven engagement acceleration. The 'unleash the potential of every team' mission has remained consistent for 20+ years under founder-led management, and the decision to bundle Rovo free for enterprise users demonstrates conviction in long-term platform value. Net-zero emissions target by 2040, 100% renewable electricity since FY2020, and the Atlassian Foundation's 1% pledge model provide ESG credibility [3].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on GAAP profitability, competitive threats, and valuation sustainability. GAAP metrics are unflattering: ROE of -19.29%, profit margin of -3.50%, and P/E of -105.78 reflect significant stock-based compensation expense that dilutes shareholders [5]. D/E of 141% is elevated for a software company. While non-GAAP margins are strong at 34%, the gap between GAAP and non-GAAP profitability raises questions about the true economic cost of equity compensation. Microsoft's Azure DevOps, Teams, and Copilot integration create a formidable bundled competitor — enterprises already paying for Microsoft 365 face lower marginal cost to adopt Microsoft's collaboration tools [2]. AI-native project management tools like Linear, Notion AI, and GitHub Copilot Workspace could erode Atlassian's developer tools moat from below. The Data Center to Cloud migration, while strategically correct, creates revenue transition risk as some customers may not convert. FY2026 guidance of ~24% total revenue growth is strong but represents deceleration from Q3's 32% YoY pace, and maintaining growth rates above 20% becomes arithmetically harder as the $6B+ revenue base scales [1][4].
key_catalysts: Rovo AI platform monetization inflection — currently bundled free for enterprise users, future premium AI features and increased seat expansion driven by 14M+ monthly assisted actions and 7x agentic automation growth could unlock significant incremental revenue [2]

Cloud migration completion driving margin expansion — as remaining Data Center customers convert to cloud subscriptions, operating leverage and recurring revenue predictability should improve, with non-GAAP operating margin already at 34% [1]

FY2026 guidance reaffirmation of ~24% total revenue growth and ~26.5% cloud revenue growth, with Q3 FY2026 delivering 32% YoY revenue growth and cloud crossing $1.132B quarterly — demonstrating acceleration above guidance [1][4]
key_risks: GAAP profitability remains negative with -3.50% profit margin and -19.29% ROE, driven by significant stock-based compensation that dilutes existing shareholders — the persistent gap between GAAP and non-GAAP metrics masks the real economic cost of equity grants [5]

Microsoft bundling threat — enterprises already paying for Microsoft 365 face minimal incremental cost to adopt Azure DevOps, Teams, and Copilot, potentially pressuring Atlassian's pricing power and new customer acquisition in large enterprise deals [2]

Revenue growth deceleration risk as the $6B+ revenue base scales — FY2026 guidance of ~24% growth represents a deceleration from Q3's 32% pace, and maintaining 20%+ growth becomes arithmetically harder, which could compress the forward multiple if the market prices in slower growth [1][4]
sources: Atlassian Q3 FY2026 Earnings Release — $1.132B cloud revenue, 32% total revenue growth, 34% non-GAAP operating margin, BusinessWire, April 30, 2026
Atlassian Rovo AI Platform and Team '26 Announcements — 14M+ assisted actions, 7x agentic automation growth, 75% Fortune 500 adoption, atlassian.com, Forrester Research, Newsweek, 2025-2026
Atlassian Sustainability Report and Corporate Social Responsibility — 100% renewable electricity, net-zero by 2040, Atlassian Foundation 1% pledge, atlassian.com, 2024
Atlassian Q2 FY2026 Earnings Release — Revenue growth and FY2026 guidance, BusinessWire, February 2026
Yahoo Finance — TEAM Key Statistics and Financial Data, accessed June 2026
